old_claim.note: Cinematography & Motion Pictures provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Industrial cinema distribution is a network for film prints, projection, and exhibition, so motion-picture production and projection are the required underlying medium.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica describes early motion-picture production and distribution by companies such as Pathé and Gaumont, including Pathé and Gaumont dominance of pre-World War I motion-picture production/distribution.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source grounds early cinema distribution in motion-picture production and distribution firms and their film-output networks.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A distribution network for film prints and exhibitions cannot exist without motion-picture capture/projection and film output, so the edge is required rather than generic enabling.

ontology_before: Industrial Cinema Distribution treated motion pictures as a generic enabling context.
ontology_after: Industrial Cinema Distribution requires cinematography and motion pictures as the underlying medium being printed, rented, projected, and exhibited.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: industrial_cinema_distribution -> cinematography_motion_pictures is now a required edge.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to pre-film optical entertainments rather than motion-picture film distribution.
A stronger source shows the scoped distribution network did not distribute motion-picture film prints or exhibitions.
A more precise film_exchanges node is added and becomes the direct required substrate.
short_reason: Cinema distribution requires the motion-picture medium it distributes.
